text.title: TAM/SAM/SOM, Competitive Table, and Value Proposition Canvas
text.summary: Market sizing with three-method triangulation, competitive intelligence table, and VPC fit check rules for agent-produced artifacts.

rule.statement: Triangulate market size with three methods (top-down, bottom-up, competitor-based); do not average if the spread is greater than 2x — report the range and flag the divergence instead.
rule.rationale: Averaging incompatible methods produces false precision. A 2x spread signals that one method's assumptions are wrong; averaging hides the problem and misleads investors.

list.item: Top-down: TAM = total industry size from reports; SAM = TAM * % matching our criteria; SOM = SAM * realistic capture rate
Bottom-up: TAM = # potential customers * average revenue per customer; SAM = # reachable customers * ARPU; SOM = # we can acquire * ARPU
Value theory: TAM = value created by solving the problem; SAM = value for specific segment; SOM = value we can capture

rule.statement: Round aggressively — $8.3B is fake precision, $8B is honest. Use two significant figures maximum for TAM/SAM.
rule.rationale: Market sizing compounds errors across four multipliers. Reporting four significant figures implies an accuracy that the underlying data does not support.

rule.statement: Cap competitor analysis at 5-7 named direct players plus one explicit "long tail" row; tag the rest as "long tail" with an estimated count.
rule.rationale: Agents over-list adjacent products to appear thorough. A table of 20 competitors is less useful than a tight table of 6 with deeper analysis per row.

list.item: Features: what do they offer? what is missing? — source: product pages, demos, reviews
Pricing: how much? what tiers? — source: pricing pages, sales calls, G2
Positioning: who do they target? what message? — source: homepage, ads, case studies
Technology: what stack? what integrations? — source: job postings, BuiltWith, tech blogs
Customer feedback: what do users love/hate? — source: G2/Capterra reviews, support forums
GTM strategy: how do they acquire customers? — source: SimilarWeb traffic, Meta Ad Library, Ahrefs

rule.statement: Check VPC fit against actual user quotes (cite interview ID per row), not assumed pains/gains — agents over-claim fit when both sides of the canvas are invented.
rule.rationale: A VPC always "fits" if the agent invented both customer profile and value map. Requiring interview citations per pain-reliever and gain-creator row forces the canvas to be grounded in evidence.

list.item: Customer profile (right): customer jobs (functional/emotional/social), pains (obstacles, risks, bad outcomes), gains (desires, success criteria)
Value map (left): products and services offered, pain relievers (how you eliminate pains), gain creators (how you create gains)
Fit check: do pain-relievers address top-3 pains? do gain-creators fulfill top-3 desired gains? does offering help with important jobs?

antipattern.description: Declaring VPC "fit" without checking pain-relievers against top-3 pains from actual interviews.
antipattern.reason: VPC fit is always partial in reality. Over-claiming fit before launch leads to underinvestment in validation and a false sense of readiness.
